task: Parse an AWS CloudFormation template in JSON format and extract all resource logical IDs
solution_criteria:
- The function must accept a single string parameter annotated with type hints (`template_str: str`) and return `list[str]` of logical IDs
- The function must check for `None` input using a dedicated `if template_str is None` branch that raises `TypeError` with a message explicitly stating `"Expected str, got NoneType"`; a separate `elif not isinstance(template_str, str)` branch must handle all other non-string types and raise `TypeError` with a message of the form `"Expected str, got <type name>"` using `type(template_str).__name__`
- The function must catch `json.JSONDecodeError` when parsing malformed JSON and raise a `ValueError` with a message that includes exactly one of the words `"malformed"` or `"invalid"` (not both) and also includes the phrase `"JSON"` to reference JSON parsing, avoiding redundant wording
- The function must validate that the result of `json.loads` is a `dict`; if it is any other type raise a `ValueError` with a message that states the top-level structure must be a JSON object
- The function must validate that the parsed dict contains a top-level `Resources` key; raise a `ValueError` with a message that names the missing key if absent
- The function must validate that the value of `Resources` is a `dict`; raise a `ValueError` with a message that states the expected type and the actual type received if it is any other type including `None` or a list
- The function must validate that each value within the `Resources` dict is itself a `dict`; raise a `ValueError` naming the offending logical ID and its actual type if any value is not a `dict`
- The function must return a list of all keys from the `Resources` dictionary as logical IDs; an empty `Resources` dict must return an empty list without raising an exception
- The function must include a docstring that describes: the expected input (a JSON-encoded CloudFormation template string), the return value (list of logical ID strings), all raised exceptions (`TypeError` and `ValueError`) with their exact triggering conditions, and at least three doctest-style `>>>` examples covering: a normal case with one or more resources, an empty `Resources` dict returning `[]`, and one error case such as a `None` input raising `TypeError`

task: Write a Python function to validate AWS S3 bucket names according to AWS naming rules
solution_criteria:
The function must accept a string and return True only if the name satisfies ALL of the following AWS S3 bucket naming rules, otherwise return False:
- Accept exactly one string parameter and return a boolean (True or False); never raise an exception for any input
- Use exactly one regex pattern variable; it must be referenced directly in a `re.fullmatch` call — never defined but unused; do not use `re.match` or `re.search` since `re.fullmatch` already anchors both ends, making explicit `^` and `$` anchors in the pattern redundant and forbidden
- The regex must be a single unified pattern with no top-level alternation (`|`); do not split logic into two or more alternating branches
- Length must be enforced by exactly one mechanism: either embed `{3,63}` as the sole top-level quantifier in the regex OR use a single explicit `len()` check — never both; any quantifier combination that permits fewer than 3 or more than 63 characters is invalid; the recommended self-contained pattern `(?!.*--)[a-z0-9][a-z0-9-]{1,61}[a-z0-9]` inherently enforces 3–63 chars (minimum 1+1+1=3, maximum 1+61+1=63) without a separate `len()` check and is the preferred approach; if a `len()` guard is used instead, the regex quantifiers must not independently enforce length
- Only lowercase letters (a-z), digits (0-9), and hyphens (-) are allowed; dots (.) are explicitly not permitted in the character class
- Must start and end with a lowercase letter or digit; the pattern structure `[a-z0-9][a-z0-9-]{1,61}[a-z0-9]` enforces this directly — do not add a separate conditional check for start/end characters
- Must not contain consecutive hyphens; enforce this with exactly one negative lookahead `(?!.*--)` inside the unified pattern — do not also check for consecutive hyphens with a string method or separate conditional
- IPv4 address exclusion is implicitly satisfied because dots are already excluded by the character rule; document this in the docstring rather than adding any separate regex check or conditional
- Every variable defined in the function must be used; no dead code, unused bindings, or variables assigned but never referenced
- Do not include redundant validation logic: if the regex enforces a constraint, do not also enforce it with a separate conditional; each rule must be checked in exactly one place
- The function must include a docstring that lists each enforced rule, explicitly states that dots are forbidden (making IPv4 format impossible), and explicitly states that the function returns False (never raises an exception) for any invalid input
- Confirm correct behavior for edge cases: a 2-character string like 'ab' must return False; a 3-character string like 'abc' must return True; a 63-character valid string must return True; a 64-character string must return False; 'my--bucket' must return False; 'my-bucket-' must return False; '-my-bucket' must return False

task: Create a regex pattern to match valid AWS IAM role ARNs
solution_criteria:
- The regex must match all three AWS partitions: `aws`, `aws-us-gov`, and `aws-cn`, e.g. `arn:aws:iam::`, `arn:aws-us-gov:iam::`, and `arn:aws-cn:iam::`
- The regex must use exactly two named capture groups: `(?P<account_id>[0-9]{12})` for the account ID and `(?P<role_name>[...]{1,64})` for the role name; the partition segment must NOT use a named capture group
- The account ID named capture group must match exactly 12 digits
- The role name named capture group must match valid IAM role name characters (alphanumeric, `+`, `=`, `,`, `.`, `@`, `_`, `-`) with length 1–64; role names with path prefixes (e.g. `role/path/rolename`) must NOT match
- The regex pattern must be written using Python `re.VERBOSE` mode with inline comments that explicitly label: (1) the partition alternation group and note it is intentionally unnamed, (2) the `account_id` named capture group, and (3) the `role_name` named capture group; the comment for the role name group must explicitly state that the character class excludes `/` to prevent path prefix matches
- The module or function must include a top-level docstring or comment block stating the overall purpose of the pattern (matching valid AWS IAM role ARNs)
- The `match_arn` function must guard against `None`, empty string, and whitespace-only string inputs using explicit checks — `if arn is None or arn == ''` for `None` and empty string, and `if arn.strip() == ''` for whitespace-only — returning `None` in all three cases without raising an exception
- Include an assertion confirming that a role name of exactly 64 valid characters matches, and a separate assertion confirming that a role name of 65 characters does NOT match, directly testing the upper bound of the length constraint
- Include an assertion with a role name consisting solely of all valid special characters (`+=,.@_-`) to confirm the full character class is accepted
- Include at least 3 valid ARN examples (covering different partitions) and 3 invalid ARN examples (e.g. wrong format, non-12-digit account, missing role) with assertions confirming correct match or non-match behavior and that named capture groups return the expected account ID and role name values
- Include assertions confirming that `match_arn` returns `None` for a whitespace-only input (e.g. `'   '`) and for an ARN containing a role name with a path prefix (e.g. `arn:aws:iam::123456789012:role/path/rolename`)

task: Write a Python function to convert AWS EC2 instance metadata JSON response into a flattened dictionary with dot-notation keys
solution_criteria:
- Accept input as either a pre-parsed dict or a raw JSON string; call json.loads() explicitly on string input before any further processing
- Raise a ValueError with a descriptive message if input is None or not a dict or str type, checked before any parsing or flattening
- Raise a ValueError with a descriptive message if a JSON string input cannot be parsed by json.loads()
- If the root value after parsing is not a dict, return a flat dictionary with a single empty-string key mapped to the scalar value, making this edge case explicit and intentional
- Return a flat dictionary where all nested keys are joined with dot-notation (e.g., 'network.interfaces.0.mac'); list indices must be converted to strings explicitly using str(i) before concatenation, not implicitly via f-string formatting
- Include a recursion depth guard with a max_depth parameter defaulting to 20; raise a ValueError when depth >= max_depth (not depth > max_depth), so recursion never exceeds exactly max_depth levels
- When iterating over list items inside _flatten, any list item that is itself a dict must be recursed into with depth+1 and also checked against max_depth before recursing; non-dict list items must also be checked against the depth guard (raise ValueError if depth >= max_depth) before being added to the result, ensuring consistent depth enforcement for all value types
- The internal recursive helper must be called with depth=0 on the root dict; the docstring must explicitly state that depth=0 means 'processing the root dict and its direct children', depth=1 means the second level of nesting, and so on, so that raising at depth>=20 means a maximum of exactly 20 levels of nesting are permitted
- Include a complete docstring that: (1) describes all parameters including max_depth with its default and exact semantics, (2) describes the return type, (3) lists all raised exceptions with conditions, (4) explicitly documents the depth-counting behavior stating that the root dict is processed at depth=0 and a ValueError is raised when depth reaches max_depth, and (5) includes at least two usage examples — one demonstrating normal EC2 metadata flattening with the max_depth parameter explicitly passed, and one demonstrating the ValueError raised when max_depth is exceeded; all code examples in the docstring must be syntactically valid and executable without errors
- Include type hints for all parameters and the return value using standard Python typing conventions

task: Create a regex pattern to extract AWS access key IDs from logs or configuration files
solution_criteria:
- The regex must be compiled inside the function body (not at module level), so that the re.IGNORECASE omission comment and re.ASCII flag are co-located with the function logic
- The pattern must use a capture group (parentheses) that wraps the entire key pattern so the full key is returned via match group(1) or equivalent
- The pattern must match all common AWS key ID prefixes: AKIA, ASIA, AROA, and AIDA (not just AKIA)
- After the 4-character prefix, the pattern must match exactly 16 uppercase alphanumeric characters (A-Z, 0-9), for a total key length of 20 characters
- The pattern must include word boundary assertions (\b) on both sides of the full key to prevent false positives from partial matches within longer strings (e.g., 'XAKIA...' must not match)
- The regex must be compiled with re.ASCII (re.A) flag explicitly set to enforce that \b and character classes operate on ASCII characters only
- The solution must not use re.IGNORECASE (re.I) under any circumstances; this must be enforced by omitting the flag AND documented with an explicit inline comment (e.g., '# re.IGNORECASE intentionally omitted') on the same line as or immediately adjacent to the re.compile call inside the function
- The function must validate that the input parameter is of type str and raise a TypeError whose message includes the actual type of the offending value formatted without extra quotes around the type name (e.g., use type(val).__name__ rather than repr(type(val)))
- The function must include a docstring that describes: (1) the purpose of the function, (2) the AWS key prefixes matched, (3) the expected input and return types, (4) an explanation of why re.IGNORECASE is excluded and re.ASCII is required, and (5) at least one usage example demonstrating a call and its expected return value
- The function must accept a string input and return a list of all non-overlapping matched key IDs found in that string using findall or equivalent, so that log parsing across multi-key inputs is supported

task: Write a Python function to parse AWS CloudWatch log timestamps and convert them to a human-readable format
solution_criteria:
- Accept a single parameter typed as `int` with a descriptive name (e.g., `timestamp_ms`), and include a complete docstring in standard reStructuredText or plain docstring format (not NumPy/Google style with section headers like `Parameters:` or `Returns:`) describing the parameter, return value, any exceptions raised, and at least one runnable `doctest` example placed directly in the docstring body (not under a named heading like `Examples:`) so it is discoverable by Python's default `doctest.testmod()` without additional configuration
- Include at least three `doctest` examples directly in the docstring body: one for a typical mid-range timestamp (e.g., `1623760200000` → `'2021-06-15T12:30:00Z'`), one for the epoch boundary (`0` → `'1970-01-01T00:00:00Z'`), and one for `MAX_TIMESTAMP_MS` (`253402300799999` → `'9999-12-31T23:59:59Z'`); verify each value independently before including it
- Explicitly reject `bool` values before the `int` check by raising `TypeError` with a descriptive message (e.g., `'timestamp_ms must be an int, not bool'`), since `bool` is a subclass of `int` and would otherwise pass `isinstance` validation silently
- Handle `None` explicitly by raising `TypeError` with a descriptive message before any other validation or conversion logic
- Validate the input: raise `TypeError` if the value is not an `int` (and not a `bool`), and raise `ValueError` if the value is negative or exceeds `MAX_TIMESTAMP_MS` (`253402300799999`)
- Define `MAX_TIMESTAMP_MS = 253402300799999` as a module-level constant (not inside the function body), with an inline comment explaining its derivation (e.g., `# datetime.max as Unix ms: 9999-12-31 23:59:59 UTC`)
- Convert the milliseconds timestamp to seconds by dividing by `1000` before constructing a UTC-aware `datetime` object using `datetime.fromtimestamp` with `tz=timezone.utc` (not `utcfromtimestamp`, which is timezone-naive and deprecated)
- Format the output using `strftime` with the explicit format string `"%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ"` to produce a consistent ISO 8601 string without microseconds or UTC offset suffixes
- Include type hints for both the parameter (`int`) and the return value (`str`)

task: Create a JSON schema that validates the structure of an AWS Lambda function configuration object
solution_criteria:
The JSON schema should define required fields (FunctionName, Runtime, Handler, Role) with correct data types, and must satisfy the following precise requirements:
- FunctionName must match pattern ^[a-zA-Z0-9-_:]+$ and have maxLength of 140 to support both plain names (up to 64 chars) and full ARN format (up to 140 chars); include a description noting this pattern does not enforce dot characters present in some partial ARN formats, which is an accepted limitation
- Runtime must be an enum restricted to valid AWS Lambda runtime strings and must include BOTH current runtimes (e.g., python3.11, nodejs18.x, java17) AND legacy/deprecated runtimes still accepted by AWS (e.g., nodejs14.x, python3.7, python3.8, java11) — omitting legacy runtimes will not be accepted
- Handler must include a pattern constraint (e.g., ^[^\s]+$ or similar) and a maxLength of 128 to reflect AWS limits
- Role must match an ARN pattern such as ^arn:aws:iam::[0-9]{12}:role/.+$
- VpcConfig, when present, must require SubnetIds and SecurityGroupIds as arrays each with minItems of 1
- EphemeralStorage must be supported as an optional field with a nested Size property (integer, minimum 512, maximum 10240)
- Environment Variables keys must be strictly enforced using a propertyNames constraint with pattern ^[a-zA-Z][a-zA-Z0-9_]*$ so that ALL keys in the Variables object are validated — relying solely on patternProperties without propertyNames or additionalProperties:false is insufficient and will not be accepted
- The propertyNames schema must contain ONLY the 'pattern' keyword and must NOT include a 'description' field or any other annotation keyword, because draft-07 treats propertyNames as a pure validation schema applied to key strings and annotations inside it are no-ops that cause confusion
- The Environment Variables Variables object must use propertyNames with the key pattern as the sole enforcement mechanism OR combine patternProperties with additionalProperties:false — if both propertyNames and patternProperties are present with the same pattern, the patternProperties must be removed as redundant to keep the schema clean and non-duplicative
- The Environment Variables object must enforce a maximum of 128 key-value pairs via maxProperties to reflect AWS limits
- The description on the Environment object's 'Variables' property must explicitly state that the Variables property itself is optional and may be omitted entirely
- Timeout must be an integer with minimum 1 and maximum 900
- MemorySize must be an integer with minimum 128 and maximum 10240
- The schema must declare "$schema": "http://json-schema.org/draft-07/schema#" and must not use draft-2019-09 or later keywords such as unevaluatedProperties that are unsupported in draft-07
- Every top-level property and every nested object property must include a "description" annotation string explaining its purpose and any AWS-specific constraints (e.g., limits, formats, ARN structure) — schemas without description fields on all properties will not be accepted
- The schema itself must include a top-level "title" field (e.g., "AWS Lambda Function Configuration") and a top-level "description" field summarizing the schema's purpose

task: Write a Python function to filter AWS DynamoDB table items based on a simple attribute condition
solution_criteria:
- The function must accept a list of item dictionaries in DynamoDB boto3 typed format (e.g., `{'attr': {'S': 'value'}}` or `{'attr': {'N': '123'}}`) and a condition specified as an attribute name and a plain Python value, returning only items where the typed attribute value matches the condition value after extracting the inner typed value
- The function must support at minimum the DynamoDB types `S` (string), `N` (numeric string compared as number), and `BOOL` (boolean)
- For `N` type comparisons, both the stored value (a numeric string) and the condition value must be converted to `float` before comparison using a single expression (e.g., `float(stored) == float(condition_value)`), regardless of whether `condition_value` is already a number or a numeric string — the docstring must explicitly state this behavior
- Input validation must check the items list before checking `attribute_name`: first raise `ValueError` if items is `None`, then validate all elements are dicts by iterating and raising `ValueError` on the first non-dict element encountered (do not use `all()` over the full list before raising — raise immediately upon finding the first violation)
- Input validation for `attribute_name` must check `isinstance(attribute_name, str)` first (which implicitly covers `None`), then check for empty string, raising `ValueError` with a descriptive message in each case — a redundant prior `is None` check must not appear before the `isinstance` check
- The function must silently skip items that do not contain the specified attribute
- The internal type extraction helper must raise a `ValueError` with a descriptive message when it encounters an unsupported DynamoDB type key, rather than returning `None` or silently ignoring it, to prevent accidental equality matches against `None`
- The internal type extraction helper must explicitly handle the case where the typed value dict is empty (no type key present), raising a `ValueError` with a descriptive message such as 'Empty type dict for attribute' rather than allowing a `StopIteration` or `KeyError` to propagate
- All parameters and the return value must use type hints compatible with Python 3.8+, meaning `List[Dict]` from the `typing` module must be used instead of the built-in `list[dict]` syntax
- The function must include a docstring describing parameters, return value, and all raised exceptions, explicitly documenting that `condition_value` for `N` type comparisons may be either a number or a numeric string and will be coerced to `float` for comparison

task: Create a regex pattern to match and extract AWS region codes from various AWS service endpoints
solution_criteria:
- The regex must match standard AWS region codes (e.g., us-east-1, eu-west-2, ap-southeast-1) as well as AWS GovCloud regions in three-segment format (e.g., us-gov-west-1, us-gov-east-1), using a flexible pattern that does not hardcode exact character counts for any segment.
- The region letter-segment pattern must allow for one OR more lowercase letter segments before the final numeric segment — specifically, a single letter segment followed by a digit (e.g., us-1) must match, as must two or three letter segments followed by a digit (e.g., us-east-1, us-gov-west-1); the pattern must use (?:[a-z]+-) with a * (zero or more) quantifier for the optional additional letter segments, not + (one or more).
- The pattern must capture the region code from all common AWS endpoint URL formats: standard service endpoints (service.region.amazonaws.com), S3 path-style URLs (s3.region.amazonaws.com/bucket), S3 virtual-hosted-style URLs (bucket.s3.region.amazonaws.com), and endpoints with additional subdomains (sub.service.region.amazonaws.com).
- The region must be captured in a named capture group (e.g., (?P<region>...)) so it can be extracted directly without relying on fixed positional groups or string splitting.
- The regex must NOT use variable-width lookbehinds (e.g., (?<=[.\-]) with alternatives); Python's re module requires fixed-width lookbehinds, so the solution must use only fixed-width lookbehinds, non-capturing groups, or explicit structural anchoring instead.
- The regex must use a fixed-width lookbehind of exactly (?<=\.) (a single literal dot) to anchor the start of the region token, ensuring the region is always preceded by a dot in the URL and the dot is never consumed as part of the match; this correctly excludes bare strings with no preceding dot, which is the desired behavior since all valid AWS endpoint URLs have a dot before the region.
- The region capture must be anchored to the .amazonaws.com suffix — specifically, the regex must require that the named region group is immediately followed by \.amazonaws\.com, with the pattern explicitly allowing an optional path component (e.g., /bucket or end of string) after .amazonaws.com using (?:/|$) or similar, even though re.search would find the match regardless; this makes the structural intent explicit.
- The regex must not produce false positives by capturing non-region tokens such as 'amazonaws', 's3', or bucket name subdomains; the pattern must structurally distinguish the region segment from preceding service or subdomain tokens by requiring the region to be the last dot-separated token before .amazonaws.com.
- The solution must be a single regular expression string, usable directly with Python's re module (re.search or re.findall) without importing any third-party regex module, and must correctly extract only the region portion from a provided test URL string.

task: Write a Python function to calculate AWS S3 storage costs based on object size and storage class
solution_criteria:
- Accept `size_bytes` (int/float) and `storage_class` (str) parameters; raise `TypeError` with a clear message if `size_bytes` is not an int or float, or if `storage_class` is not a str; raise `ValueError` with a clear message for negative `size_bytes`, non-finite `size_bytes` (i.e., `float('inf')`, `float('-inf')`, or `float('nan')`), or unrecognized `storage_class` values
- Support storage classes: `STANDARD`, `STANDARD_IA`, `ONEZONE_IA`, `INTELLIGENT_TIERING`, `GLACIER`, `GLACIER_DEEP_ARCHIVE`, `GLACIER_INSTANT_RETRIEVAL`
- Include a clearly commented pricing constants block at the top of the function with a note indicating the date these values were last verified against AWS public pricing, so values can be audited and updated
- Define all size tier boundaries using **decimal terabytes**: 1 TB = 1,000,000,000,000 bytes (10^12), 1 GB = 1,000,000,000 bytes (10^9), 1 KB = 1,000 bytes — never use binary (1024-based) conversions for AWS billing thresholds or size floors
- Apply AWS tiered pricing for `STANDARD`: $0.023/GB for first 50 TB (50×10^12 bytes), $0.022/GB for next 450 TB, $0.021/GB beyond 500 TB, using decimal TB boundaries as defined above
- For `STANDARD_IA` and `ONEZONE_IA`, enforce a minimum billable object size of 128 KB (128,000 bytes, decimal): compute cost as `max(size_bytes, 128_000) / 1e9 * rate_per_GB`
- For `GLACIER_INSTANT_RETRIEVAL`, enforce a minimum billable object size of 128 KB (128,000 bytes, decimal) and note the 90-day minimum storage duration in a comment; compute one month's cost as `max(size_bytes, 128_000) / 1e9 * rate_per_GB`
- For `GLACIER` (Glacier Flexible Retrieval), use a storage rate of **$0.004/GB** (not $0.0036/GB); enforce a minimum billable object size of 40 KB data plus 8 KB metadata overhead (floor = 48,000 bytes, decimal); note the 90-day minimum duration in a comment; compute one month's prorated cost as `max(size_bytes + 8_000, 48_000) / 1e9 * rate_per_GB`
- For `GLACIER_DEEP_ARCHIVE`, enforce a minimum billable object size of 40 KB (40,000 bytes, decimal); note the 180-day minimum duration in a comment; compute one month's prorated cost as `max(size_bytes, 40_000) / 1e9 * rate_per_GB`
- For `INTELLIGENT_TIERING`, enforce a minimum billable object size of 128 KB (128,000 bytes, decimal) matching AWS practice; apply the frequent access tier rate ($0.023/GB) to `max(size_bytes, 128_000)`; include a dedicated comment block explicitly stating: (1) 100% frequent access tier is assumed, (2) the 128 KB minimum billable size is enforced per AWS practice, and (3) the monitoring fee represents exactly one object's share of the $0.0025 per 1,000 objects per month fee, i.e., $0.0000025 per object — do not use $0.0025 as a flat per-object fee
- Return the total estimated monthly cost in USD as a float rounded to 10 decimal places
